topic: Process - Command Builder and Child Process

turns.content: I'm trying to port my C shell script to Rust, but I thought the best way was to just use std::process::Command with a single string argument like "ls -l" so it would parse everything automatically.

That is actually not how Command works. You must pass the command as a separate String and then provide each argument individually via the args method on the builder, otherwise the shell will interpret your Rust arguments as shell metacharacters instead of passing them directly to the program.
Oh okay, so I should do Command::new("ls").arg("-l")? But how does that actually start the process and what happens if I just call .spawn()?

Yes, you chain arg calls on the builder. When you call spawn(), it returns a Child object which is an owned handle to the running process. However, if you only have the Child handle without waiting for it to finish or checking its output, your program might exit before the command completes.
Right, so I need to wait on that Child handle? Is there a simple way to check if it finished successfully?

You call wait() on the Child instance. It blocks until the process exits and returns an ExitStatus along with any output. If you want to know if it succeeded without blocking, you can use spawn_no_wait() which returns Ok(Child) or Err on failure immediately.
Wait, isn't there a simpler way? Like just calling .status()? I thought that was the main method.

No, status() is not part of Command; you must call wait() to retrieve the ExitStatus. There is no direct status getter on the builder because the process hasn't started yet until you invoke spawn or spawn_no_wait.
Got it, so I always get a Child object back? And what if I need to send some input into that command's stdin?
Yes, spawn returns an owned Child. To write to the standard input of the spawned process, you create a pipe using std::process::Pipe and set it as the stdin on the Command builder before spawning.
I tried piping but now I'm confused about output too. Should I also pipe stdout? How do I read from that?

You can pipe both stdin and stdout by configuring multiple pipes in the Command builder, then spawn returns a Child with those pipes attached. You must manually read from each pipe using std::process::Pipe::read_to_end or iterate over bytes if you need streaming.
That sounds complicated for my first port. What about environment variables? Do I set them after spawning the child?
No, you configure envs on the Command builder itself before calling spawn. You cannot modify the environment of a process that has already been spawned; those settings are fixed at construction time.
Okay so if I need to change the current working directory for the command? Is there a method for that too?
Yes, you can set the current working directory on the Command builder using the method named after the trait item, which sets the cwd before execution.
One more thing, what if I just want to kill a running process without waiting? Is there a way to send a signal like SIGTERM?
You can use the kill() method on the Child object after you spawn it. This sends a signal to terminate the process immediately without needing to wait for an exit status.
So my workflow is: build command with args, envs, cwd, pipes -> spawn -> optional kill or wait -> read outputs? That makes sense now.

Exactly. You construct the builder with all your options, invoke spawn to get the Child handle, optionally send signals via kill(), and then either wait for completion or manually read from the attached pipes.
Wait, I realized something else. If I pass a command like "cat" but no arguments? Does Command require at least one argument after the name?

No, the first String is always the executable itself, so passing just one argument means that single string is treated as the program to run. You only add extra args via the arg() method if you need additional parameters.
Perfect! So I can do Command::new("cat") and it will work? And what about error handling if the command isn't found?

Yes, Command::new takes a single String as the executable. If that path doesn't exist or is not in PATH, spawn will return an Err variant containing details about why the process could not be started.

topic: Process - Command Builder and Child Process

turns.content: I'm building a CLI tool in Rust using std::process::Command but I keep getting the wrong output because my shell is interpreting parts of my command string incorrectly. How do I pass arguments safely without relying on shell expansion?

The core issue here is that you are likely passing your arguments as a single string to Command instead of using the args field, which expects a collection of String values. When you use a raw string, the operating system's shell interprets spaces and special characters before Rust even gets control. You should construct your command by providing the program name separately from a list of individual arguments so that Rust handles the parsing rather than the environment. Think of it like sending ingredients to a chef versus telling them to go buy groceries yourself; if you give them a list of specific items (args), they just prepare the meal exactly as requested, whereas if you describe the whole process in natural language (a shell string), they have to interpret your intent and might make mistakes. The correct approach is to use Command::new with the program path followed by separate arguments in the args parameter. Before we proceed further, are you currently running this on Windows or Linux?

It's Linux right now. I managed to fix it by passing args as a Vec<String>, but now I need to set environment variables for a specific child process so that it can access files in the parent directory.

You have successfully separated the arguments, which is critical for safety. To inject environment variables into your child process without affecting the parent's environment, you should use the envs parameter on Command::new, passing in a map of String to String pairs. This ensures that only the specific variables you intend are available to the spawned Child process while keeping the parent isolated from accidental overwrites or pollution. A helpful analogy is setting up a dedicated workspace for your child; you provide it with a specific toolkit (the envs) so it can work without needing access to everything in the main room, preventing conflicts between processes.
That makes sense. However, I'm noticing that my child process seems to be exiting immediately after starting and never actually runs any code. What's going wrong?

The most likely culprit is that your command might not have a working path or you are trying to spawn the wrong executable. When Command::new spawns a program, it creates a new Child instance which represents the process tree node. You must ensure the program name in the command string actually exists and is executable before attempting to wait on its status. If you are using relative paths like 'my_app', verify that my_app is in your current_dir or the parent's working directory. Additionally, check if your code inside the child process is actually valid Rust syntax; sometimes a compilation error causes the spawn to fail silently depending on how you handle errors.

I checked and the path exists. I realized I might be confusing stdout redirection with stdin piping. How do I pipe input from my parent process into the command's standard input stream so it doesn't wait for EOF?

When using Command::new, you can enable input streaming by setting the stdio field to a specific mode like Stdio::piped, which returns two handles (stdin and stdout) that your Rust code can use to read or write bytes. To pipe data from your parent process into the child's stdin, you must call the handle provided by Command after it has been spawned via spawn. You cannot do this before spawn because the stream doesn't exist yet until the Child is created. Think of it like opening a phone line; you can't make a call before the line is actually established. Once you have the handles from spawn, you use them to write your input data into stdin and read output from stdout.
Okay, I see now that I need to wait for completion first. But what if my child process needs to run indefinitely? Is there a way to keep it alive without waiting on its exit status?

By default, you call Child::wait to block until the process terminates and retrieve its exit status. If your application requires the child to remain running while you manage its lifecycle separately, you should avoid calling wait immediately after spawn. Instead, store the Child instance in a variable and only invoke wait when it is safe to do so or when the process has finished its task. This allows you to keep the process alive as long as needed, using kill if necessary later to terminate it gracefully.
I understand now. I'm trying to implement error handling too. How do I catch specific errors from Command::new and distinguish between a missing executable versus an argument parsing failure?

Command::new returns Result<Command, std::io::Error>, which allows you to inspect the error source directly. You can use match or if statements to check whether the error is caused by file system issues like permission denied or path not found versus other problems. However, argument parsing failures typically happen later during execution and are caught via Child::wait returning an Err with the exit status code indicating failure. The Command stage mainly validates that the program exists and the environment variables map is valid.
That clarifies things. One last thing, how do I retrieve the exact exit code after a successful wait so I can determine if the child process failed?

After calling Child::wait successfully, you receive an ExitStatus which contains the integer exit code and potentially signal information. You should unwrap or match on this value to extract the exit status code as a u8 or i32 depending on your needs. This gives you precise control over how to interpret the result of the child process termination.
